stories.body: Iran’s military said it fired on U.S. Patriot batteries in Kuwait, an early-warning site in Qatar, a fuel depot in Bahrain and Jordan’s Azraq base in retaliation for American raids that Tehran says killed 14 people and injured 78 across five provinces as the late Supreme Leader Ayatollah Ali Khamenei was laid to rest in Mashhad, while U.S. Central Command said it had just hit about 90 Iranian targets to keep the Strait of Hormuz open amid an unraveling ceasefire, surging oil jitters and mutual threats of escalation.
stories.headline: Iran claims strikes on U.S. Gulf bases as Khamenei buried

stories.body: Three sources close to the Kremlin told Reuters that President Vladimir Putin has rejected advisers’ calls for a ceasefire and is poised to escalate the five-year-old war by prioritizing full control of Ukraine’s Donbas and possibly targeting NATO-linked sites, a stance hardened by Ukrainian drone strikes on Russian refineries and ports that have triggered fuel shortages, domestic discontent, and more intense Russian missile barrages on Ukrainian cities.
stories.headline: Kremlin insiders see Putin preparing fresh offensives despite U.S. peace push

stories.body: Iranian Foreign Minister Abbas Araqchi insisted a regional truce with the United States must include an Israeli withdrawal from southern Lebanon and a ceasefire with Hezbollah, reinforcing Tehran’s pledge to keep supporting the Lebanese group even as clashes with Israel and U.S. naval interventions keep the Strait of Hormuz largely shut, global oil prices elevated and humanitarian agencies warning of rising hunger from disrupted trade.
stories.headline: Iran links any U.S. deal to Lebanon ceasefire and backs Hezbollah

stories.body: U.S. Central Command said its forces shot down four Iranian drones it believed were targeting commercial shipping near the Strait of Hormuz before striking surveillance radar at Goruk and Qeshm Island, the latest exchange in a war that has largely halted traffic through the vital waterway even as Washington and Tehran pursue indirect talks over sanctions relief, oil revenue access and maritime security, with President Donald Trump claiming Iran retains only about 22% of its missiles.
stories.headline: U.S. hits Iranian coastal radar after downing drones near Hormuz

stories.body: UNESCO reported that 113 developing countries paid more on servicing foreign debt than on educating children in 2025—spending 3.6 times more in sub-Saharan Africa and up to 16 times more in Sri Lanka—while aid to education has already fallen 21% since 2023 and could drop 30% by 2027, prompting calls for long-term debt relief structures that prevent private creditors from blocking restructurings and allow governments to fund schools and teachers.
stories.headline: UNESCO: Debt payments now outstrip education spending in most developing states

stories.body: Typhoon Bavi triggered evacuations of more than 14,000 people in Taiwan as heavy rain and strong winds swept the island on Saturday, following power outages across Japan’s Okinawa chain and with forecasters warning that the storm will continue toward eastern China after battering coastal infrastructure and transport links.
stories.headline: Typhoon Bavi forces thousands to evacuate in Taiwan after lashing Okinawa

stories.body: The U.S. Senate voted 52-47 along party lines to add $70 billion for Immigration and Customs Enforcement and Border Patrol over the next three years but rejected efforts to permanently kill former President Donald Trump’s $1.8 billion “anti-weaponization” fund for compensating allies, setting up a contentious House fight as Republicans juggle internal dissent, Democratic criticism of the so-called slush fund, and Trump’s plan to nominate acting Attorney General Todd Blanche to lead the Justice Department.
stories.headline: Senate advances $70 billion ICE funding while sparing Trump ally fund

stories.body: Relatives of Lorenzo Salgado Araujo, a longtime Houston resident without criminal convictions, demanded an independent investigation after an Immigration and Customs Enforcement officer fatally shot him on July 7 while he was driving co-workers to a construction site, disputing agents’ claims that Salgado tried to ram them with his van and drawing support from a Texas congresswoman for outside scrutiny.
stories.headline: Family seeks independent probe after ICE officer kills Houston construction worker

stories.body: Maine Democrat Graham Platner suspended his Senate campaign days after POLITICO reported a new allegation that he forced sex on Jenny Racicot nearly five years ago, a claim he denies, but which nonetheless cost him the backing of national progressive figures, party committees and major donors, leaving Democrats scrambling to pick a replacement before the July 27 deadline to challenge Republican Senator Susan Collins in November.
stories.headline: Progressive Graham Platner quits Maine Senate race amid assault allegation

stories.body: The Seventh Circuit Court of Appeals reinstated Illinois’ 2023 Protect Illinois Communities Act, ruling 2-1 that restrictions on AR-15-style rifles, large-capacity magazines and related attachments align with the nation’s historical firearm regulations, reversing a lower-court decision and prompting Governor JB Pritzker and Attorney General Kwame Raoul to hail the ruling while gun-industry plaintiffs vowed to appeal to the U.S. Supreme Court.
stories.headline: Appeals court upholds Illinois ban on semiautomatic weapons

stories.body: Current and retired judges speaking across the Midwest sounded alarms about far-right rhetoric, threats of violence and partisan campaigns aimed at delegitimizing the judiciary, urging lawmakers and the public to defend court independence amid rising security concerns and online harassment of jurists.
stories.headline: Judges warn of threats and politicized attacks during Midwest tour

stories.body: Authorities said flames that ignited Thursday near Los Gallardos, Almería, have burned roughly 4,000 hectares of rugged scrubland, destroyed isolated homes and left at least 12 people dead with 23 still unaccounted for, marking Andalusia’s deadliest wildfire on record as emergency crews battle uncontrolled fronts.
stories.headline: Los Gallardos wildfire kills at least 12 with 23 missing in Almería

stories.body: The Guardia Civil confirmed it is investigating whether a dropped electrical cable sparked the Los Gallardos fire after early witnesses reported vegetation igniting beneath power lines, with the Andalusian government saying what began as a roadside blaze has become a lethal inferno now tied to the region’s infrastructure.
stories.headline: Fallen power line probed as trigger for deadly Almería blaze

stories.body: Spain’s Civil Guard elite investigative unit asked National Court Judge Santiago Pedraz to authorize access to phones seized from PSOE general manager Ana Fuentes, already a defendant in the Leire Díez-SEPI corruption case, and two other party employees as agents follow money trails tied to suspected influence peddling involving state holding company contracts.
stories.headline: UCO widens ‘Leire Díez’ probe to PSOE finance staff

stories.body: Spanish far-right party Vox has brought back historian Fernando Paz—who resigned as 2019 Albacete lead candidate after questioning the Nuremberg trials and endorsing gay conversion therapy—by featuring him at youth events, appointing him as a lecturer in a party-linked school and naming him an honorary patron of a nationalist foundation, moves that critics say underscore the party’s radicalization.
stories.headline: Vox reinstates candidate accused of Holocaust revisionism

stories.body: Barcelona’s Generalitat infrastructure chief Ramon Ramírez said residents evacuated after an eight-meter-wide sinkhole linked to Line 9 tunneling in the Putxet neighborhood must remain out of their homes for another week while technicians complete subsoil scans, though inspections so far show stabilized structures and the tunnel-boring machine keeps inching forward under strict monitoring.
stories.headline: Putxet residents displaced by L9 tunnel collapse told to stay out until July 17

stories.body: Geographer and former deputy mayor Jordi Borja, who helped Pasqual Maragall decentralize Barcelona into districts, draft the city charter and later co-found Barcelona en Comú after years in exile and service with the PSUC, died Friday at age 85, prompting tributes for his decisive role in shaping the modern city.
stories.headline: Urban planner Jordi Borja, architect of Barcelona’s district model, dies at 85

stories.body: An indefinite strike launched May 26 by Barcelona’s library workers, led by CGT and Intersindical unions over staffing and wage demands, kept roughly 26.8% of branches closed in early July—down from 78% during the first week—but continues to disrupt key climate shelters as the city council skipped a mediation session while saying talks remain active and unions plan to maintain pressure through summer and possibly intensify it in autumn.
stories.headline: Barcelona library strike shuts a quarter of branches during heatwave
